text: Yes, the eternal problem of having to use existing technology in order to get anything done and then being limited by those technologies. My current view is that we have to accept these dynamics and thus accept that all progress in complex technology is incremental. That doesn’t make it less frustrating, but at least I can stop thinking about it.

elements.text: Yes, the eternal problem of having to use existing technology in order to get anything done and then being limited by those technologies. My current view is that we have to accept these dynamics and thus accept that all progress in complex technology is incremental
That
doesn’t
make it less frustrating
but at least I can stop thinking about it.

text: I enjoyed this article on <http://wingolog.org/archives/2024/01/08/missing-the-point-of-webassembly|Missing the Point of WebAssembly>. Count me as one of the people previously "missing the point" by this definition. I now feel less inclined to explore wasm in my web dev work, and more inclined to explore it in my VPL work.

elements.text: I enjoyed this article on
Missing the Point of WebAssembly
. Count me as one of the people previously "missing the point" by this definition. I now feel less inclined to explore wasm in my web dev work, and more inclined to explore it in my VPL work.

text: The article reminded me of the nice concept of <https://www.oilshell.org/cross-ref.html?tag=narrow-waist#narrow-waist|narrow waists>, which is exactly what WebAssembly is according to the author. My impression is that this is more a promise than a reality at the moment, but... wait and see.

elements.text: The article reminded me of the nice concept of
narrow waists
, which is exactly what WebAssembly is according to the author. My impression is that this is more a promise than a reality at the moment, but... wait and see.

text: In case you’re not familiar, Tchernavskij’s follow-up work in his thesis (<https://theses.hal.science/tel-02612943>) goes beyond Webstrates.

elements.text: In case you’re not familiar, Tchernavskij’s follow-up work in his thesis (
) goes beyond Webstrates.

text: Thanks for sharing the paper <@U8A5MS6R1>!  I completely agree about the limitations of building on top of the current Web model. I've never seen Webstrates as a solution, it's our vehicle to explore ideas that's difficult to explore with conventional software.

elements.text: Thanks for sharing the paper

!  I completely agree about the limitations of building on top of the current Web model. I've never seen Webstrates as a solution, it's our vehicle to explore ideas that's difficult to explore with conventional software.

text: Niels Olof Bouvin and I also did a shortpaper for hypertext where we experimented with some old hypermedia principles on top of Webstrates: <https://klokmose.net/clemens/files/classical-hypermedia.pdf>

elements.text: Niels Olof Bouvin and I also did a shortpaper for hypertext where we experimented with some old hypermedia principles on top of Webstrates:

text: I've for a long time wanted to explore how to make a 'proper' hypermedia system with qualities like the ones you point out <@U8A5MS6R1>. A good place to start for inspiration is the Dexter Reference Model for Open Hypermedia (<https://dl.acm.org/doi/abs/10.1145/175235.175237>), but as far as I remember, it doesn't address the type of dynamism you talk about.

text: We've multiple times talked about how we can have a more general transclusion model in Webstrates, e.g., supporting partial transclusion but it turns out to be quite a fundamentally challenging problem on many levels.

elements.text: We've multiple times talked about how we can have a more general transclusion model in Webstrates, e.g., supporting partial transclusion but it turns out to be quite a fundamentally challenging problem on many levels.

text: That’s been my dream! It’s just too large to learn from scratch, and when I found a half dozen embeddable VMs, I couldn’t get any of them to work. :man-facepalming::skin-tone-4:

elements.text: That’s been my dream! It’s just too large to learn from scratch, and when I found a half dozen embeddable VMs, I couldn’t get any of them to work.

text: I think the author is over emphasizing web assembly's novelty though ... if you take the para starting "But WebAssembly is a new point in this space ..." and substituted LLVM/JVM/CLR/... you'd end up with an equally reasonable result.
